content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. There Will Be Blood

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Just finished watching this movie after seeing a suggestion in of the sub's post and man, Am I glad I did! 
Went in totally blind without even checking imdb and wasn't disappointed.  If you're looking for a movie suggestion, yeah, I totally recommend this one. Added to the list of the best movies I've watched in a while.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I would say it is the best movie after 2000 
Just a masterpiece
  - Yeah honestly, it’s up there for me too. Day-Lewis just ate that role
2. Well its one of the best all time movie. If ***Paul Thomas Anderson*** wasn't enough on his own as director and script writer, it has the amazing ***Daniel Day Lewis*** in one of his all time best acting role too.

**Must watch for any cinephile**!!
  - I think DDLs performance in that film is the best acting ever. I mean he wasn't acting - he was Daniel Plainview.

I'm serious.
    - Well that is what **Daniel Day Lewis** does!! But I would argue that nothing is the "Best". There are other very good actors too. They just don't do epic roles as DDL. But I definitely agree that he is special.
3. The bowling pin scene at the end. Just wow. 

Paul Dano and DDL are so great in this.
  - "I'm finished." Cut to Brahms Violin Concerto in D Major. Standing ovation.
4. DDL is a beast
5. One of my fav movies and DDL at his best! Well he does the job pretty much all the time.
6. It's definitely in my top 5 films of all time.

## 2. Suggest me a movie about geniuses (ie. A Beautiful Mind, Goodwill Hunting, Hidden Figures)

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I love movies about geniuses/gifted people being discovered or doing amazing things. Example movies:

A Beautiful Mind  
Goodwill Hunting  
Hidden Figures  
Gifted Hands: The Ben Carson Story
Rain Man

  
Thanks.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Real Genius
2. Searching for Bobby Fischer (1993)

Finding Forrester (2000)

See what I did there?
3. Two about mathematicians 

The Man Who Knew Infinity - about the fascinating story of mathematician Srinivasa Ramanujan

the Imitation Game about Turing during WW2 breaking the enigma code.
4. My left Foot , not directly a genius but a guy with cerebral palsy learns himself to paint and write with the only part of his body he could control, his left foot is pretty impressive and it is a great movie as well
5. *Amadeus* (1984)
6. Little Man Tate
7. Old, but moving. Based upon the science fiction story "Flowers for Algernon" - Charly (1968), with an academy award winning best actor performance.
8. Phenomenon
9. 'Real Genius' (1985) with Val Kilmer.
10. Matilda (1996 & 2022)

## 3. Gritty and grimy crime/thrillers

**Score:** 11
**Body:** I love really gritty, bleak movies. Some of my favourites are Narc, 8mm, shot caller, prisoners, Dragged across concrete and good time.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 'The French Connection' (1971)

'To Live and Die in LA' (1985)

'The Night of the Juggler' (1980)

'Serpico' (1973)

'The Driver' (1978)

'Bad Lieutenant' (1992)

'Reservoir Dogs' (1992)

'Pusher' trilogy (1996 - 2005)
2. Blue Ruin

Joe (2013)

Love & A. 45

Mud
3. Prince of the City (1981) - "*A New York City narcotics detective reluctantly agrees to cooperate with a special commission investigating police corruption, and soon realises he's in over his head, and nobody can be trusted.*"
4. LA Confidential

Seven

Heat

Taxi Driver

No country for old men
5. I Saw The Devil
6. Sicario
7. *The Chaser* is fantastic and extremely bleak.
8. "Ms .45" (1981)

Bad Lieutenant (1992)

"Prince of the City" (1981)

"The Star Chamber" (1983)

"The Drop" (2014)

"Night Hawks" (1982)

"The Game" (1997)

"The Driver" (1978)

"Point Blank" (1967)

"Pusher" (1996)

"Pusher 2" (2004)

"Pusher 3" (2005)

"Irreversible" (2002)

"Sympathy for Mr. Vengeance" (2002)

"OldBoy" (2003)

"Lady Vengeance" (2005)

"Stay" (2005)

"I Saw the Devil" (2010)

"Summer of Sam" (1999)
9. *Dark Blue* (2002)
10. King of New York

Bad Lieutenant 

## 4. Delusional Captain

**Score:** 12
**Body:** Hello, do you have any recommendations for movies that revolve around a character that can be described as a delusional captain. Whether the character literally has the title captain or is metaphorically captaining in any way. :)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Caine Mutiny (1954). It is a great classic movie
2. Event horizon, any iteration of moby dick, apocalypse now, mutiny on the bounty, sunshine.
  - Apocalypse Now was an iteration of Conrad’s Heart Of Darkness.
3. Zaphod Beeblebrox

Hitchhikers guide to the Galaxy
4. Galaxy Quest
5. Captain Ron told them to watch out for gorillas, in the Caribbean.  
6. Little Big Man, not the main character, but Custer was a pretty big character and pretty damn delusional.
7. Fight Club (1999)
8. Crimson Tide, bay-bee
9. A few good men (1992)

High-Rise (2015)

## 5. Looking for cozy but slightly spooky movies for Halloween night 🎬

**Score:** 13
**Body:** Hey! I’m in the mood for movies that feel cozy but still have a bit of a spooky vibe — something Halloween-y without being super scary. Stuff like Coraline, Practical Magic, Hocus Pocus, or Halloweentown.

Any chill, slightly spooky movies you’d recommend for a cozy night in? 🍂

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Haunted Mansion (2003)
  - That one’s perfect for the cozy-spooky vibe!
2. Addams family movies, casper, monster house, beetlejuice, tower of terror, frankenweenie, corpsebride, 9, wallace and gromit the curse of were rabbit, gremlins
  - Beetlejuice and Monster House hit that sweet spot of spooky but not nightmare fuel haha
3. Edward Scissorhands 
4. A Nightmare Before Christmas
5. Over the Garden Wall (series)
6. Halloween Tree

Monster Squad

Ernest Scared Stupid
  - Hahaha came here to suggest Ernest Scared Stupid. That movie had me when I was a kid!
7. The Addams Family (1991)

Beetlejuice (1988)

Casper (1995)

The Burbs (1989)

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What final line in a movie still haunts you to this day with how profound it is? I'll go first:

**Score:** 1155
**Body:** Se7en (1995)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "You met me at a very strange time of my life..."
2. Shutter Island 

“What would be worse, to live as a monster or die as a good man?”
3. Oh, I wish we could continue our chat…but I’m having an old friend for dinner.
4. “ I hope I can make it across the border. I hope to see my friend and shake his hand. I hope the Pacific is as blue as it has been in my dreams. I hope." - Shawshank Redemption
  - I almost wish the movie ended like that, but I can’t hate on seeing the happy reunion
5. "I know you're out there. I can feel you now. I know that you're afraid. You're afraid of us. You're afraid of change. I don't know the future. I didn't come here to tell you how this is going to end. I came here to tell you how it's going to begin. I'm going to hang up this phone, and then I'm going to show these people what you don't want them to see. I'm going to show them a world without you. A world without rules and controls, without borders or boundaries. A world where anything is possible. Where we go from there is a choice I leave to you."

-Neo, The Matrix
6. In The Princess Bride… grandpa to grandson “as you wish.”
7. The greatest trick the devil ever pulled was convincing the world he didn't exist. And like that... he's gone.
8. "Men like my father cannot die. They are with me still - real in memory as they were in flesh, beloved and loving. How green was my valley then."
9. "Had dreams... Two of 'em. Both had my father in 'em. It's peculiar. I'm older now then he ever was by twenty years. So, in a sense, he's the younger man. Anyway, the first one I don't remember too well but, it was about meetin' him in town somewheres and he give me some money. I think I lost it.

The second one, it was like we was both back in older times and I was on horseback goin' through the mountains of a night. Goin' through this pass in the mountains. It was cold and there was snow on the ground and he rode past me and kept on goin'. Never said nothin' goin' by - just rode on past. And he had his blanket wrapped around him and his head down. When he rode past, I seen he was carryin' fire in a horn the way people used to do, and I-I could see the horn from the light inside of it - about the color of the moon. And in the dream I knew that he was goin' on ahead and he was fixin' to make a fire somewhere out there in all that dark and all that cold. And I knew that whenever I got there, he'd be there. And then I woke up."

## 2. What’s a famous movie where your ACTUAL favorite scene isn’t the most talked about scene?? Mine is when Ashley Judd gives Val the signal that it’s not safe to come home in heat

**Score:** 1104
**Body:** I mean the Al/Rob scene or the shootout at the bank robbery gets all the love but this I think is my favorite moment in the movie. 

Just the shot up and battered Chris (Val) looking up and seeing the love of his love give him the subtle signal that it’s not ok to come home just kills me. The both of them in that moment just knowing they are never again gonna see the love of their life is just heartbreaking 

So yeah it made me wonder…. Are there any huge movies like that where the moment that makes them huge isn’t what you like best 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. IT’S THE SMELL! -Agent Smith
  - If there is such a thing.
2. Gladiator. 

The most quoted is the Maximus line, but Marcus Aurelius has a better quote I think. 

“I am dying, Maximus. When a man sees his end... he wants to know there was some purpose to his life. How will the world speak my name in years to come? Will I be known as the philosopher? The warrior? The tyrant...? Or will I be the emperor who gave Rome back her true self? There was once a dream that was Rome. You could only whisper it. Anything more than a whisper and it would vanish... it was so fragile. And I fear that it will not survive the winter.”
3. Dumb and dumber- Lloyd walks out of a convenience store and tries to make small talk with some locals and it goes nowhere because he’s an idiot.

“Big gulps? Alright! …well… check you later”
4. I love the scene in Casino Royale, when Bond, after the machete fight in the stairway, goes back to his room and calms down infront of the bathroom mirror. Gives me goosebumps.
5. Actually same actress, same movie, different moment. When they are fighting and Val yells at her and you see the fear, anger, resolve and determination on her face all in the matter of a few seconds. She gets overlooked but her performance in that movie was outstanding.
  - Yeah that’s great. And yeah it’s so criminal for a bunch a reasons of course but the simple fact that Ashley was seemingly Weinstein blacklisted robbed us of two decades of performances like this one
6. Speaking of Heat... when Bobby D finds Trejo all fucked up and Trejo (can hardly speak) very quietly asks to be finished off (in a round about way).

That one is weirdly thrilling.
7. "Lance, the goddamn phone is ringing!"
"I can hear it."
"Thought you told those fucking assholes and never to call here this late?"
"I did. And that is exactly what I'm going to tell this fucking asshole right now."
That exchange before one of the most intense scenes in the movie just tickles me to no end!
8. Dude, such a great choice. They were toxic as fuck but man that was some real love there. 

Mine is predator when Dutch confronts Dylan. As Dylan is explaining why he got them there his eyes are tearing up. Its breaking his heart that he has to tell his best friend that he was expendable. He very possibly thought that they'd win & he wouldn't have to get his buddy killed, or he thought they'd all die together. A small nuance I love about predator.

## 3. What movie refused to give audiences what they paid for?

**Score:** 507
**Body:** For Halloween Ends, the movie promised a final battle between Laurie and Michael, instead, the movie focuses mostly on Corey Cunningham. I think Corey is an interesting character, but he's not who we paid to see. We paid to see Laurie and Michael fight, which did happen ... for about 2 minutes. And it was a pretty lame fight in my opinion.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Presumably people might have felt this abput Psycho. Thought they were getting a thriller starring Janet Leigh all the way through...
  - I went to a 35mm screening of Psycho last year and during the interval, I heard a guy behind me say "I had no idea she died so early on."
2. I paid to see Goodfellas but I don't think any of those fellas are good
  - You’re a funny guy.
    - Funny, like I'm a clown?
3. Snow Dogs (2002). The trailers made it seem like it was a comedy with talking dogs. I was a really pissed off 9 year old coming out of that movie theater.
  - Same with Kangaroo Jack.
4. Alien vs Predator requiem designed a great Predalien but made the movie so dark you can barely even see it.
5. The Neverending Story
6. The poster looks like zombie Kevin Bacon.

## 4. I just watched A House of Dynamite and are you actually fucking kidding me with that “ending”?!?!?

**Score:** 408

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What ending?
  - Surrender or suicide, mutually assured destruction, that's the ending.
2. I liked it because it made me realize that this is the *second* time Jared Harris has played a man having a meltdown because he can't get his daughter out of a city about to be hit by a nuclear warhead.
  - Not great, not terrible.
    - 3.6 roentgen
      - It’s not 3.6 roentgen. It’s 15,000.
3. My brother and I, both being former military, called bullshit on the seat of your pants of firing off a nuke when you don't know who did it.

Also we have a nuclear missile alert system. It would have gone out nation wide, then once the selected city was pinpointed, it would have gone off again letting those in the city know.

Also, samples taken from ground zero, once analyzed, can pinpoint which reactor was used to make the fissile material.

Purpose of the film was to show how fucked things are in a crisis.
  - Agree with you 100% re the purpose and the facts you state, but unfortunately, the film's plot sins took me way out of it to the point that the overarching message was lost (or, rather, diluted).

1.  Zero rationale for POTUS to have a ticking clock to retaliate on these facts.  None.  Absolutely contrived.

2.  No way they would only send 2 interceptors after the first ICBM attack on us, and before anyone responds, the film presented firing two as almost lackadaisical, with keeping the rest in reserve "because."  Yeah, I agree keep a lot in reserve, but IRL they are firing at least a half dozen unless technically impossible, which was *not* *remotely* what was presented in the film, i.e. there was no technical limitation presented that limited them to 2 interceptors.  One could argue this demonstrates the bigger point of human error, but nah, too unrealistic.

It was like doing a 20 mile hike with two big pebbles in your shoe.  Maybe the destination is great, but you're going to be cranky as hell when you get there.
4. My literal reaction was “wait? What the fuck??”
5. Damn. Am I the only one that liked it? I liked the chaos in it. The uncertainty of everything and how nobody really knows what’s going on. I would think in reality, that’s kind of how it would go. If the movie had the ending that everyone wanted, it would have gone into a whole other direction and that would be another movie in itself.

## 5. We can all agree that Swayze was the fucking man!

**Score:** 383

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Back off war child, seriously.
  - Back off Anthony Keitus.. I'm serious
2. He was awesome in Point Break and Roadhouse.
  - And the legendary hockey movie, Youngblood.
    - And The Outsiders
  - Yes and Dirty Dancing and Ghost.
3. I love that most of the sky diving was faked for insurance purposes but that Patrick and his brother went up on their own and filmed it live.
  - He received da cease and dessist letter from the studio as he was involving a lot of the crew in living out his character in real life, and the execs were shitting bricks fearing an injury lawsuit or scandal.
4. REDDAWN! DONT CRY!!!
5. “I’m not gonna paddle to New Zealand!”

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. How do I study cinema on my own?

**Score:** 79
**Body:** Hey guys, I want to start studying cinema as a hobby — not in an academic way, just because I really love movies and want to understand them better.

In general, what should I actually do to become more educated about cinema and it's history?

Should I focus on watching specific directors, movements, or time periods first? Do I read essay, watch a podcast or something? 

If anyone has essays, book recommendations, or even YouTube channels to learn from, please share! 

Thanks in advance! 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Same here so I'll tell you what I'm doing. I bought the book *Film Art: An Introduction (Bordwell & Thompson)* and I have been reading it little by little in my free time. I try to watch as many of the films mentioned. I have learned a lot so far and it has changed the way I watch films because of how much more I observe and analyze. 

I will be watching this thread for more suggestions.
  - Film Studies major here and the Bordwell & Thompson book 👆🏽was our primary text in our first intro Film History course.
2. The Story of Film by Mark Cousins (either book or TV series) gives an excellent overview of cinema history and really helped me to understand the national and historical context of some of the greats, as well as how they created precedents for what came later. Otherwise, the books of David Bordwell as others have mentioned.


On YT I really enjoy House of Tabula, Thomas Flight, Like Stories of Old, Moviewise, and Be Kind, Rewind (but that's less analysis and more Hollywood lore). A lot of people also like Patrick Willems but while I think his analyses are fine, I personally find his "comedy bits" off-putting - you may be OK with them though :)


The Blank Check podcast is good for season-long deep dives on particular directors, and I've also heard good things about "You Must Remember This" though I haven't listened yet.
3. I can only offer an academic perspective: when I started my Film studies degree James Monaco's book 'How to read a film' was suggested as a starting point by my professors. I think it is  a good introduction to a a lot of themes, also it does reflect on different film theories throughout history. Someone suggested Thompson/Bordwell, which is per se great, but it has already a  specific film theory behind it.

Other than that, if you have a local film museum or a programm Cinema, visit their educational programmes!
4. Not necessarily the same, but I teach high school English and one of the things I’ve noticed with teaching kids to read is that learning to write themselves is an essential part to it. 

If you have the time, even in the smallest way, I might try to film something yourself to get a better understanding of what it really means to make a film.
5. Get a subscription to Criterion and then watch a bunch of great films and then google why they are great and do your research. I think the best way to study film is to watch as much of it and as many genres of it as possible.
  - Seconded, criterion channel has expanded my horizons so much. They do occasional collections based on country, filmmaker/actor, era ... great way to see things you normally wouldn't seek out.
6. Buy books and read them. Start off with a general history book and maybe a book of classic film recommendations. Check out James Monaco’s How to Read a Film and Bordwell & Thompson’s Film Art. Work through collections of reviews by critics like Pauline Kael and Roger Ebert and watch the movies in question. If you are in college take a class on film theory and criticism. 

Watch a lot of classics from every movement and era and attempt to kill whatever biases you think you may have. Most importantly go out of your way to see actual 35mm film projected. Video is a sad approximation of the art form.
7. The Story of Film: An Odyssey
(2011)  Highly recommend this series!

I never liked reading essays or analyst on films, unless I've already seen it & interested in learning more about the production.  I prefer to form my own opinions first.

I've learned the most by watching film with the commentary on (usually only on dvd's).

Also learned a lot from watching a director/actor's entire body of work. Their first & last films usually show an interesting arc.
8. I would start with general cinema history, find a book about it, and as you read it go watching the movies that are cited, doesn't have to be all of them, but enough to get an idea of the period or movement. After you finish it you'll have a better idea of the general scope, then you can find which gender/movement/country interest you and study them more in deph.

I'm a little skeptical of youtuber and podcasters, for sure there are some good ones, but there's also a lot of them that have no idea of what they're talking about it. I still watch them occasionally but with the mindset that's is just someone giving their opinion not necessarily a learning resource.

## 2. Wake in Fright (1971) Thirst as a condition

**Score:** 30
**Body:** # [Bundanyabba: threshold, fever, release](https://horscadrecinema.substack.com/p/wake-in-fright-1971-thirst-as-a-condition)

*“The desert is not a place. It is a verdict.”*

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vk3H!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1ef7f3c6-9ff7-4128-9981-6b611d4de052_1920x1080.png)

There are films that unfold like itineraries and films that lower the air in the room until every breath carries weight, *Wake in Fright* belongs to the second kind, the kind that refuses the comfort of plot as progress and draws you instead into a condition where the horizon judges, hospitality behaves like gravity, and accepting a beer becomes the first step into a ritual that asks for nothing and takes everything. John Grant arrives in Bundanyabba with a teacher’s arithmetic in his head, days left on contract, pounds saved, miles to Sydney, the promise of a woman waiting, the idea of a life about to resume. Within a handful of gestures the arithmetic comes apart, not because the town waves a weapon at him, but because it welcomes him so completely that refusal feels like an insult to the order of things.

Ted Kotcheff films that welcome with a foreigner’s clarity. The Canadian eye is unenchanted by national myth and uninterested in exotic display, it looks and the act of looking becomes an x-ray. The pub is a room with clean lines and heavy air. The streets are open yet airless. The policeman’s smile is genuine and loaded with expectation. A coin turns on a green baize table and, for a second, it contains the logic of the place inside its spin. Nothing announces itself as a trap. That is why the trap works.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!w3wY!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F738b7999-6db2-4c45-a5bf-04e672ae7004_1920x1080.png)

**the Yabba and the grammar of liminal spaces**

Long before the internet taught us to name those eerily familiar non-places that feel like déjà vu with the lights on, Bundanyabba was staging their grammar in plain sight, a civic mirage that looks like a town and behaves like a threshold that never opens. Once inside, there are two movements available to anyone who wants to remain human. Accept the void and dissolve into its rituals until nights become indistinguishable. Or move through every state it induces and step out marked but intact. John Grant’s path reads like the entire spectrum: courteous initiation where hospitality feels like safety, loosening where repetition replaces choice, vertigo in which empathy thins and the body obeys the room, the shame that follows morning, and finally the lucid passage that is not victory so much as the decision to live with what he now knows about himself. If you reach back carefully, a distant cousin exists in *Carnival of Souls*, where derelict halls feel like verdicts on a refusal to belong. Kotcheff replaces gothic pallor with outback glare, yet both films trap their protagonists in spaces that look open and function like closed rooms, forcing a choice between absorption and emergence.

If Bundanyabba functions as a liminal zone, and the film insists upon this with ruthless patience, it is because everything inside it operates like a doorway: pub thresholds, train platforms, the literal desert roads where tires draw meaningless lines across an infinity of dust, even the faces of the men who welcome John with an affection that burns, all of them thresholds with no obvious second room, a logic [Du Cinéma](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IFAqSEv429c) would call dream-core because the spaces feel remembered rather than seen, familiar in the wrong way, as if the mind had invented them to explain a feeling it could not otherwise name. Kotcheff keeps returning to the most ordinary thresholds, the door to the pub, the door to the room, the door to the house where Doc Tydon waits. And the more often John crosses them, the less they resemble passage and the more they resemble a test.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!iU5L!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5d8d47a2-2575-4ea4-9d99-ba5131806142_1200x645.png)

Australia appears less as geography than as a public subconscious, with the Yabba as one of its pockets where appetite, habit and belonging arrange themselves into law.

It is tempting to call the desert empty. Kotcheff films it as density. A flatness so absolute it exerts pressure. A sky so wide it presses down rather than opening up. Every road out of town looks like relief until a few miles reveal only repetition and heat, and **the realisation that thirst is not a symptom, it is a condition**. On that wavelength the film converses with Nicolas Roeg’s *Walkabout* where city and outback are incompatible states of consciousness, with *Picnic at Hanging Rock* where landscape swallows certainty and leaves a residue of awe and fear, with *Long Weekend* where nature quietly tallies small violations until the debt must be paid, with *The Thirst* and later *Next of Kin* where suburbia and isolation start to breathe like minds under strain. Each work recognises Australia not as location but as a field of forces that alter whoever steps inside.

**Masculinity as self-destruction**

The rituals binding the Yabba resemble conviviality and function as submission. Alcohol circulates not as pleasure or escape, but as a sacrament designed to dissolve doubt. The sergeant buys the outsider one drink and then another. The gambling room hums. A coin spins toward its tiny chaos. Identity loosens, not because a grand decision is made, but because repetition wears down resistance the way sand grinds glass. The grin that follows a win or the shrug that follows a loss belong to men who have learned that feeling is expensive and numbness is cheap.

Gary Bond gives John Grant a brightness that first reads as composure and then as denial. Once you see the shift you cannot unsee it. He does not plunge, he erodes. Each pour, each laugh, each night on a stranger’s floor removes a layer until the man who thought he was simply passing through discovers that he has been consenting by degrees. In the Yabba, consent is not a signature, it is a rhythm you acquire.

The kangaroo hunt sits where it must sit, not as provocation and not as allegory, but as ethical fever captured with documentary footage because fiction could not match the truth of what was witnessed. Bodies stumble and slam into trunks. Animals fall badly and suffer. Morning arrives with metal in the mouth. The camera does not look away and the film refuses the narcotic of condemnation. Silence becomes the loudest sound. The thesis is set without speech. Empathy is rarely lost in a single act; it is removed one gesture at a time until no one notices it has gone.

Doc Tydon, played by Donald Pleasence with a slouched intelligence that has curdled into fatalism, makes this state visible. He calls himself tramp and philosopher, both accurate within the town’s logic. Knowledge has not liberated him. It has dissolved will. Books and bottles share shelves. Boundaries melt when identity is under siege. The film refuses labels and lectures. It observes the ease with which surrender can feel like wisdom when other forms of wisdom fail.

There is a woman who looks at John and sees a line out. Not rescue, not consolation. A recognition that the Yabba divides men into those about to be absorbed and those who might still leave. Her disappointment when she realises he is no exception has the force of prophecy. The town is not a collection of brutes. It is an ecology of stuckness. Imagination is the rarest resource. Men like Doc are what the ecology produces when quick minds decide the weather inside them is permanent climate.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!NMmJ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F02b1813d-d333-4690-a20f-f74ed579cc8a_1920x1080.png)

**A cinema of entrapment and return**

Kotcheff’s lens drifts from surface into an interior hallucination that feels factual. The camera steps over ordinary thresholds and returns with images that behave like symptoms. Sound turns flies, glass, chair legs and breath into instruments. Scenes end not on punch lines, but on the instant where staying one minute longer would mean agreeing to live here forever.

A comparison across oceans clarifies the cosmology. In John Boorman’s *Deliverance* the land punishes arrogance and survivors crawl back toward a civilisation that promises to restore the self. In *Wake in Fright* the land offers no parable and the town offers no absolution. Roads and vehicles do not remove danger. They carry you toward the room where you finally decide whether to accept responsibility for the things you do when warmth and agreement are easy.

**Why this film changed Australian cinema**

Situating Wake in Fright within the surge of Australian cinema that followed clarifies its innovative force. Where Nicolas Roeg’s ***Walkabout*** stages the desert as a site of re-education through contact with another way of being, and ***Picnic at Hanging Rock*** turns the landscape into metaphysical absence, and ***Long Weekend*** imagines nature as an ethical counterforce silently totting up the debt owed to it, Kotcheff and writer Evan Jones use the town as a distillation device, concentrating the cultural alcohol until its proof becomes unbearable. The result helped open a corridor for films as different as ***Mad Dog Morgan*** (feral outlaw myth scraped down to madness), ***The Thirst*** (civilization as a thirst that cannot be slaked), and later ***Next of Kin*** (suburbia and isolation as mutually reinforcing hallucinations), each of them taking a shard of Wake in Fright’s vision; the country as a pressure that prints itself onto the psyche, and pushing it toward horror, crime opera, or supernatural dread. If you come to this lineage through ***Not Quite Hollywood***, the documentary’s infectious energy makes clear how one film’s moral fever gave permission for a whole cinema to stop flattering its audience and start interrogating it.

*Wake in Fright* broke the colonial habit of flattering the gaze and turned it inward until it found nerves.

The industrial story mirrors the thematic one. The film left local audiences uneasy and box office indifferent, vanished into storage and rumour, then returned when the original negative was recovered and restored. Cannes 2009 answered with a standing ovation. Quentin Tarantino spoke of a “moral fever”. Programmers who had circled the text for years finally named it. The pattern recalls the slow afterlives of *Donnie Darko* or *Jacob’s Ladder*, works that found their public once the culture became the audience they required.

**Spectre of Success**  
**Budget:** \~800,000 AUD  
**Box Office (initial domestic):** Mixed, limited commercial success  
**International Run:** Minimal, quickly withdrawn  
**Negative:** Lost for decades, rediscovered in a Pittsburgh vault  
**Restoration:** Premiered at Cannes in 2009 to a standing ovation  
**Current Status:** Canonized as a foundational work of the Australian New Wave

**Directed by:** Ted Kotcheff  
**Director of Photography:** Brian West  
**Starring:** Gary Bond, Donald Pleasence, Chips Rafferty, Sylvia Kay

Numbers read like coordinates on a map already walked. What looked like failure now reads as foundation.

**Exit without euphoria**

John does leave. Air becomes air again. The body recalibrates. The film refuses the comfort of purification. Emergence means carrying what was seen without superiority. You pay what you owe. You acknowledge what you agreed to. You understand that leaving resets nothing, it reorients everything. The imagined poster of a future in Sydney becomes a real room that will demand decisions you can no longer outsource to climate, custom or the smile that buys the next round.

The last movement belongs to the viewer. *Wake in Fright* continues after the credits in the inventory you make of yourself. How often you drink because the room requires it. How quickly you laugh because everyone else is laughing. How readily you decide heat is reason enough to forget yourself. The men who remain become part of the town’s air, the town becomes part of the country, the country becomes a way of thinking that can live far from any desert. John’s achievement is not victory. It is recognition. The desert was already inside him.

This is why the work endures, and why it belongs at the center of Hors Cadre. The films we follow here do not decorate thought. They change its weather. They cling not as memories but as evidence. They turn space into destiny and ritual into x-ray. If the Yabba felt familiar while you read, as a street you have stood on, a room you entered, a ritual you obeyed because refusal cost too much, then you already understand the line the film draws and why it has not faded.

The door is open. The air is hot. Choose carefully.

Surely this film would elevate anything it touches just like it makes the viewer a better person, and the critic, a better critic.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Partly because he's just an elite director. Yeah, his style is more entertaining, but even beyond that, he makes high-quality films.

Id also say he does a good job of using the true events as a jumping off point to build off of.
2. I've thought about this a lot, because I'm pretty bored with biopics these days, but something like Goodfellas is absolutely a biopic and I don't think of it as being generic. I think part of it is that the subject of Goodfellas is not famous. It makes it easier to just make it a story.

Maybe that's true to some degree of most of his biopic films- They're not about people who are famous on the level of a Bob Dylan or Bruce Springsteen or Freddie Mercury, so they can't lean on the crutch of people pointing at the screen, going "That's the guy!!"

Maybe telling true stories about people who are not so recognizable avoids a lot of the traps of biopics- Sometimes biopics of famous figures feel like just an excuse for an actor to do an impression, and play some famous songs, and there's not really more to it.

You can't make a movie around just doing a Henry Hill impression or Jordan Belfort impression, you have to actually tell a good story.
  - Story and screenplay is the thing, way before any one persons amazing bio, and all the cool directorial flourishes. you need a great story to tell, period.  
  
 So if the bio has a good story in it you focus on the story inside and show that part and not the boring chronicle of a life part.. btw I keep thinking of Walk Hard as the best example here of biopic parody :)

I'd say Steve Jobs movie by Sorkin tried to do that by focusing only on 3 moments in his life (IMHO not entirely successfully but still way better than the other Jobs biopic) ie Sorkin and Danny Boyle knew that..

Goodfellas is a crazy good story, because scorsese is just good at focusing on bringing all the details around the story he is telling to life and then on top of that his detailed and intense  style and amazing cast is just icing on the cake.. you need all three for a dynamite film: great story, at least some great charismatic actors, and finally good directing style.

I felt that the dylan biopic is a classic example of a good bio but bad story..just what was the story being told? cast was great (Esp Ed Norton) and setting and style was good..but what was the story?
3. Humor, collaboration with good writers and casting directors, Thelma Schoonmaker's editing (huge part of it), using music well without it feeling overindulgent. He also doesn't over romanticize his subjects. Too many biopics put their subjects on a pedestal. Scorcese does not - nor is he afraid to do his own interpretation of who they really were
4. There are a couple of reasons. To name a positive one, Scorsese is just outstanding at character studies. It's at the centre of his whole oeuvre, including his documentaries.

But the bigger reason is that most biopics are dogshit, and they are dogshit, because most of them are big studio projects made by hired guns for huge audiences. They are costly investments aiming for the biggest return, not any artistic merit. So the studios have some schmuck turn the depicted person's life into an innocuous, vaguely inspiring, run-of-the-mill Save The Cat script - and already the movie is doomed to be dogshit.
5. He has an artistic vision and uses a persons life to express it. Most biopics are just reenactments with good acting and a straightforward narrative… or an already tested narrative structure like flashbacks etc.
6. Something like Raging Bull is definitely a biopic but it never really registered as such in my mind as such the way The Aviator did.

  - The Aviator is a standardly heavy handed mainstream biopic. I think it’s still great because I’m disposed to be really engaged the depiction real life classical Hollywood, though.
